title: 10 best herbal teas for peptic ulcers

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea contains antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties that directly address the underlying causes of peptic ulcers, such as irritation, oxidative stress, and erosion of the stomach lining. Specifically, its bioactive compounds, like apigenin and luteolin, inhibit the enzyme responsible for damaging the mucosal lining. Additionally, its soothing effects on the gastrointestinal tract promote the production of protective mucus, further contributing to the healing process. Consequently, regularly consuming chamomile tea provides consistent relief and supports stomach health, helping prevent the recurrence of peptic ulcers.
tea_parts: Flowers: The chamomile flowers contain flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, helping to reduce inflammation and damage in the stomach lining caused by peptic ulcers.
Leaves: The leaves of chamomile contain tannins that have astringent properties, helping to protect and soothe the stomach lining affected by peptic ulcers.
Seeds: The chamomile seeds are rich in essential oils, such as bisabolol and chamazulene, that possess anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, aiding in the healing process of peptic ulcers.
tea_recipe: Measure - Pour 1 cup of boiling water over 1 heaped teaspoon (2.5 grams) of dried chamomile flowers.
Steep - Let the chamomile flowers steep for 10 minutes.
Strain - Strain the chamomile flowers from the tea.
Sweeten (optional) - Add honey or sugar to taste.
Serve - Drink the chamomile tea while warm, up to 3 times a day for peptic ulcer relief.
tea_constituents: Azulene: Has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining caused by peptic ulcers.
Flavonoids: Possess anti-ulcer and anti-oxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining and promote healing of peptic ulcers.
Bisabolol: A powerful anti-inflammatory compound that can help reduce pain and inflammation associated with peptic ulcers.
Chamazulene: A compound that exhibits strong anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant effects, which can help protect the stomach lining and promote healing of peptic ulcers.
Apigenin: A flavonoid that has been shown to have gastroprotective effects, which can help prevent and heal peptic ulcers.
Matricin: A compound that is converted to chamazulene in the steam distillation process, and has anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant effects that can help with peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the Althaea officinalis plant, contains mucilage that creates a protective layer in the digestive tract. This protective layer soothes and relieves irritation in the stomach lining, reducing exposure to digestive acids. The mucilage in marshmallow tea also exhibits antibacterial properties, eliminating the H. pylori bacteria responsible for many peptic ulcers. Moreover, it promotes mucosal regeneration, accelerating the healing process of the damaged tissue. Ultimately, marshmallow tea offers a natural, effective method for treating peptic ulcers, alleviating symptoms, and fostering recovery.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in medicinal compounds, the roots of marshmallow plant produce a soothing tea that helps heal peptic ulcers due to its anti-inflammatory properties.
Leaves: High in mucilage, marshmallow leaves create a demulcent tea that coats and protects the stomach lining, easing pain and promoting ulcer healing.
Flowers: Packed with antioxidants, marshmallow flowers contribute to a healing tea that fights free radicals, supporting the stomach's defense against peptic ulcers.
Seeds: Abundant in healing properties, marshmallow seeds produce a comforting tea that alleviates symptoms of peptic ulcers, thanks to their mucilage content.
tea_recipe: Combine 1 teaspoon of dried marshmallow root and 1 cup of water in a saucepan, allowing it to steep for 10-15 minutes on medium heat.
Strain the liquid to remove the marshmallow root, keeping the golden-brown liquid for consumption.
Pour the marshmallow root tea into a cup, optionally adding honey or lemon to taste.
Allow the tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature, ensuring it's not too hot for the stomach lining.
Sip the marshmallow root tea slowly, consuming 2-3 times a day for soothing relief of peptic ulcers.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow tea coats the stomach lining, providing a protective barrier against stomach acid and helping to reduce irritation and inflammation associated with peptic ulcers.

Flavonoids: Marshmallow tea contains flavonoids, such as rutin and quercetin, which have anti-inflammatory properties and may help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, promoting healing of peptic ulcers.
Ascorbic Acid: Marshmallow tea contains vitamin C, which is important for collagen synthesis and may help in the healing process of peptic ulcers.
Phenolic Acids: The phenolic acids in marshmallow tea have anti-oxidant properties and may help reduce oxidative stress in the stomach, promoting healing of peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea provides relief for peptic ulcers due to the demulcent properties of its main component, slippery elm bark. This bark contains mucilage, a substance that turns into a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel creates a protective barrier over the mucous membrane of the digestive tract, reducing irritation and inflammation. By coating the stomach lining, slippery elm tea lessens the pain associated with peptic ulcers and encourages the healing of damaged tissues. Moreover, it has been historically used to alleviate gastrointestinal issues, including acid reflux, making it a popular natural remedy for ulcer treatment.
tea_parts: Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make medicinal tea for peptic ulcers due to its demulcent properties, which means it has a soothing and healing effect on the mucous membranes of the digestive tract.
Leaves: The leaves of the slippery elm plant are used in teas for peptic ulcers because they contain mucilage, a substance that coats and protects the stomach lining.
Seeds: Slippery elm seeds are used in teas for peptic ulcers because they contain large amounts of mucilage, which can help reduce inflammation and promote healing.
Roots: While not as commonly used as the bark, leaves, and seeds, the roots of the slippery elm plant can also be used in teas for peptic ulcers due to their demulcent and emollient properties.
tea_recipe: Measure - Using a measuring spoon, measure out 1 teaspoon of dried slippery elm bark.
Steep - Place the measured bark into a tea infuser or loose leaf tea bag, then steep in boiling water for 10-15 minutes.
Stir - After steeping, stir the tea to ensure all the slippery elm particles are well mixed.
Strain - Strain the tea through a fine mesh strainer into a cup to remove any remaining bark particles.
Drink - Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day between meals to soothe peptic ulcers.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: Slippery elm tea contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slippery gel when mixed with water. This gel coats the stomach and esophagus, protecting them from acid and providing relief from peptic ulcers.
Tannins: Slippery elm tea also contains tannins, which have astringent properties. Tannins can help to reduce inflammation and promote healing of peptic ulcers.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids are antioxidants found in slippery elm tea. They have anti-inflammatory properties and can help to protect the stomach lining, which can reduce the risk of developing peptic ulcers.
Starch: Slippery elm tea contains starch, which can help to soothe and coat the stomach. This can provide relief from the pain and discomfort associated with peptic ulcers.
Sterols: Sterols are substances found in slippery elm tea that have anti-inflammatory properties. They can help to reduce inflammation in the stomach and promote healing of peptic ulcers.

Triterpenoids: Triterpenoids are substances found in slippery elm tea that have been shown to have gastroprotective effects. They can help to protect the stomach lining and prevent the formation of peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea aids peptic ulcers due to its ability to increase the mucus lining of the stomach, which protects it from acid and digestive enzymes. This plant also contains compounds that inhibit the growth of Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium known to cause stomach ulcers. Moreover, licorice tea has glycyrrhizic acid, which stimulates the production of prostaglandins, hormonelike substances that promote healing of the stomach lining. Consequently, regular consumption of licorice tea provides relief and assists in the prevention of peptic ulcers.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which helps to increase the mucus production in the stomach, protecting the stomach lining and helping to heal peptic ulcers.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the licorice plant are rich in flavonoids that possess anti-inflammatory and healing properties, making them effective in treating peptic ulcers.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant contain various compounds, such as triterpenoid saponins, that have been shown to have anti-ulcer effects, making them useful for treating peptic ulcers.

Seeds: The seeds of the licorice plant contain high levels of flavonoids and coumarins, which have been shown to reduce gastric acid secretion and have anti-ulcer effects, making them a valuable addition to medicinal tea for treating peptic ulcers.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of chopped licorice root with 4 cups of water, then bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice pieces.
Let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 3 cups a day, between meals, for up to 2 weeks.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This triterpenoid saponin is the main active component of licorice root, and has been shown to inhibit the growth of Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium strongly linked to the development of peptic ulcers.

Liquiritin: This flavonoid is known for its anti-inflammatory and anti-ulcer properties, and it helps in promoting the healing of peptic ulcers by suppressing gastric acid secretion and increasing mucus production.
Glabridin: This isoflavan inhibits the growth of H. pylori, and exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, which can help protect the gastric mucosa and promote the healing of ulcers.

isoliquiritigenin: This flavonoid has exhibited gastroprotective effects by increasing mucus secretion, inhibiting gastric acid secretion, and scavenging free radicals, which contributes to the prevention and treatment of peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Calendula tea aids in treating peptic ulcers due to the anti-inflammatory and healing properties of calendula extract. This herbal tea contains flavonoids, which protect the gastric mucosa by increasing mucus secretion and blood flow in the stomach lining. Moreover, calendula's antimicrobial properties counteract Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium strongly linked to peptic ulcers. Additionally, calendula tea alleviates gastric acid secretion, reducing the risk of tissue damage and promoting the healing of existing ulcers. Finally, calendula's demulcent effect soothes irritated stomach tissues, further supporting the healing process of peptic ulcers.
tea_parts: Flowers: The calendula flowers contain anti-inflammatory and healing properties that help soothe the mucous membrane of the stomach, providing relief for peptic ulcers.
Leaves: The leaves of the calendula plant have antispasmodic and wound-healing qualities, making them ideal for treating peptic ulcers.
Rhyzomes: Calendula rhyzomes have a high concentration of mucilage, which forms a protective layer on the stomach lining and promotes healing of peptic ulcers.
Stems: The stems of the calendula plant contain essential oils and alkaloids that have a soothing effect on the digestive system, providing relief for peptic ulcers.
Seeds: Calendula seeds are rich in anti-oxidants that aid in the repair of damaged stomach tissue and promote the healing of peptic ulcers.
tea_recipe: To make calendula tea for peptic ulcers, first boil 2 cups of water in a pot.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried calendula flowers to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the calendula flowers.
Drink the tea while warm, 2-3 times a day, for best results.
tea_constituents: Flavonoids: These antioxidants help reduce inflammation and damage caused by gastric acid, promoting healing of peptic ulcers.
Triterpenes: Such as oleanolic and ursolic acids, they possess anti-inflammatory and cytoprotective properties, helping to protect the stomach lining and promote ulcer healing.

Carotenoids: Calendula contains carotenoids like beta-carotene, which can be converted to vitamin A. This vitamin aids in mucous membrane maintenance, promoting stomach lining health and ulcer healing.
Polysaccharides: They have been shown to stimulate immune response and aid in tissue regeneration, which can help in the healing process of peptic ulcers.
Volatile oils: These constituents, including limonene and pinene, have anti-inflammatory properties, contributing to the reduction of inflammation and pain associated with peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Geranium tea combats peptic ulcers due to its tannin and gallotannin content, which possess astringent properties. Astringent agents contract tissues, reducing gastric acid secretion and shielding the mucous membrane. Additionally, geranium tea contains antimicrobial compounds that eliminateHelicobacter pylori, a primary cause of many peptic ulcers. Furthermore, geranium tea boasts anti-inflammatory and healing effects, promoting mucosal restoration and wound healing in ulcer-damaged areas. These combined actions make geranium tea an effective aid in treating peptic ulcers.
tea_parts: Roots: Geranium roots contain tannins that have astringent properties, helping to reduce inflammation and promote healing of peptic ulcers.
Leaves: The leaves of geraniums are rich in flavonoids, known for their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which can help treat and soothe peptic ulcers.
Flowers: Geranium flowers contain oils that have antibacterial properties, helping to eliminate the H. pylori bacteria that can cause peptic ulcers.
Seeds: Geranium seeds are a source of essential fatty acids, which have anti-inflammatory effects and can promote the healing of peptic ulcers.
tea_recipe: Choose and rinse: Select 2-3 fresh geranium leaves, rinse them under cold water to remove dirt or impurities.
Boil water: Pour 1 cup of water into a saucepan, bring it to a boil.
Steep geranium leaves: Place the rinsed geranium leaves in a teapot, pour the boiling water over them, let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain and serve: Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the leaves, let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Consume: Drink 2-3 times a day, 30 minutes before meals, for best results in managing peptic ulcers.
tea_constituents: Tannins: These are astringent compounds that can help to protect the mucous membranes of the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and promoting healing in peptic ulcers.
Flavonoids: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help to reduce inflammation and promote wound healing in peptic ulcers.
Terpenes: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that can help to reduce inflammation and fight H. pylori infections, which are a common cause of peptic ulcers.
Essential oil: Geranium oil has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation and fight H. pylori infections in peptic ulcers.
Polyphenols: These compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation and promote healing in peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Meadowsweet tea contains salicylate compounds, specifically derivatives of salicylic acid, which exhibit anti-inflammatory properties. These compounds inhibit the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances responsible for initiating the inflammatory response in the gut lining. By reducing inflammation, meadowsweet tea promotes healing of the damaged mucous membrane in the stomach and duodenum, providing relief from peptic ulcers. Furthermore, meadowsweet tea's astringent tannins form a protective layer over the ulcers, shielding them from digestive acids and enzymes. This dual action of meadowsweet tea - inhibiting inflammation and protecting the ulcer site - makes it an effective traditional remedy for peptic ulcers.
tea_parts: Flowers: The flowers of meadowsweet contain salicylic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce pain and inflammation associated with peptic ulcers.
Leaves: The leaves of meadowsweet are rich in tannins, which have astringent properties that can help protect the mucous membrane of the stomach and duodenum, promoting healing of peptic ulcers.

Rhizomes: The rhizomes of meadowsweet contain high levels of phenolic compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to have gastroprotective effects and can help speed up the healing process of peptic ulcers.
tea_recipe: In a mortar, crush 2 tablespoons of dried meadowsweet flowers using a pestle to release their beneficial compounds.
Transfer the crushed flowers to a tea infuser and place it in a teapot.
Pour 1 liter of boiling water over the infuser and let it steep for 10-15 minutes to extract the medicinal properties.
Remove the infuser, discard the used flowers, and allow the tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Consume 2-3 cups of meadowsweet tea per day, between meals, to help soothe and heal peptic ulcers.
tea_constituents: Salicin: A natural anti-inflammatory compound that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief for peptic ulcers.
Flavonoids: These compounds have been shown to possess gastroprotective properties, helping to prevent the formation of peptic ulcers and promoting healing of existing ones.
Tannins: These astringent compounds can help protect the stomach lining by forming a protective layer, reducing irritation and promoting healing of peptic ulcers.
Phenolic acids: These compounds have been shown to have anti-ulcer properties, helping to prevent and treat peptic ulcers by reducing inflammation and promoting healing.
Essential oils: The essential oils present in meadowsweet tea, such as methyl salicylate, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation and promote healing of peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Catnip tea soothes peptic ulcers due to its antispasmodic properties, which relax the stomach muscles, reducing pain and promoting healing. This tea, derived from the Nepeta cataria plant, contains nepetalactone that stimulates receptors in a cat's brain, leading to relaxation. Interestingly, this same compound aids humans by increasing the gastric mucus secretion that protects the stomach lining, helping ulcers to heal. Moreover, catnip tea boasts anti-inflammatory properties, further contributing to the healing process of peptic ulcers. Ultimately, the soothing effect of catnip tea on the digestive system makes it an ideal natural remedy for peptic ulcers.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, leaves are used for their anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the mucous membrane in the digestive tract.
Flowers: Containing anti-spasmodic compounds, flowers can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate pain associated with peptic ulcers.
Stems: Packed with flavonoids, stems can help strengthen capillary walls in the stomach lining and support the healing process.
Seeds: High in essential oils, seeds can help stimulate digestion and regulate acid production in the stomach, reducing the risk of ulcer formation.
Roots: With anti-microbial properties, roots can help eliminate harmful bacteria, such as H. pylori, that can cause peptic ulcers.
tea_recipe: Infuse 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves in 1 cup of boiling water for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, avoiding any sharp movements that could damage the fragile ulcerated tissue.
Let the tea cool down to a warm temperature, ensuring it's not too hot to irritate the stomach lining.
Drink two cups of this catnip tea daily, after meals, to soothe the pain and aid in the healing process of peptic ulcers.
Continue this routine for two weeks, or as recommended by your healthcare provider.
tea_constituents: Nepetalactone: Acts as a natural anti-inflammatory agent, which can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining caused by peptic ulcers.
Rosmarinic acid: Possesses antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to peptic ulcers.
Tannins: Have astringent properties that can help reduce inflammation and promote healing of the stomach lining.
Flavonoids: Have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining and promote healing.
Essential oils: Can help stimulate the production of digestive juices, which can aid in digestion and help prevent the formation of peptic ulcers.
Antacids: Can help neutralize stomach acid, providing relief from symptoms of peptic ulcers such as pain and burning.

teas.tea_desc: Agrimony tea aids in alleviating peptic ulcers due to its tannin content, which possesses antibacterial properties that combat Helicobacter pylori, a primary cause of these ulcers. The tea's ability to stimulate mucus production provides an additional layer of protection for the gastric lining, preventing further damage. Furthermore, agrimony tea contains catechins and gallic acid, which boast anti-inflammatory qualities that help reduce inflammation in the stomach. Additionally, this herbal tea promotes the healing of existing ulcers by encouraging cell regeneration in the gastric lining. Lastly, agrimony tea's astringent properties, resulting from tannins, aid in contracting and tightening stomach tissues, thereby alleviating pain and promoting overall stomach health.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in tannins, the leaves aid in reducing inflammation and providing relief to peptic ulcers.
Flowers: Containing flavonoids, flowers help heal and protect the gastrointestinal tract, beneficial for treating peptic ulcers.
Roots: Known for their astringent properties, roots assist in healing peptic ulcers by reducing stomach acidity and inflammation.
tea_recipe: In a stove-top pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5-10 minutes.
Add 2 teaspoons of dried agrimony herb to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the agrimony herb, pouring it into a cup.
Let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature, which may take around 10-15 minutes.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day, after meals, for 2-3 weeks to help soothe peptic ulcers.
tea_constituents: Tannins: These are astringent compounds that can help to reduce inflammation and promote healing of the mucous membrane in the stomach, making them effective in the treatment of peptic ulcers.
Flavonoids: These antioxidant compounds have anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from the symptoms of peptic ulcers.

Volatile oils: These oils contain compounds such as cineole and limonene, which have been shown to have antimicrobial properties effective against Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium linked to the development of peptic ulcers.
Polyphenols: These compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to protect the stomach lining from damage, reducing the risk of peptic ulcers.
Triterpenes: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties that can help to promote healing of peptic ulcers.

teas.tea_desc: Plantain tea aids in treating peptic ulcers due to its demulcent properties, which involve the formation of a soothing film over the mucous membrane lining the stomach. This film protects the lining from acid and digestive juices, reducing the risk of further damage. Moreover, plantain tea contains mucilage, a complex carbohydrate that swells when in contact with water, forming a barrier against harmful substances. Its anti-inflammatory and healing effects, attributed to the compound allantoin, further contribute to the alleviation of pain and inflammation associated with peptic ulcers. Lastly, plantain tea contains tannins, which exhibit astringent properties, helping to decrease secretions and promote tissue repair, fostering the healing process of existing ulcers.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the plantain plant contain high amounts of iridoids, which have been found to protect the gastric mucosa from damage caused by acid and pepsin, thus helping to heal peptic ulcers.
Leaves: The leaves of the plantain plant are rich in mucilage, a gel-like substance that coats and protects the stomach lining, providing relief from ulcer symptoms.
Seeds: Plantain seeds are a demulcent, meaning they soothe irritated tissues and can help reduce inflammation caused by peptic ulcers.

Bark: The bark of the plantain plant contains tannins, which have astringent properties that can help shrink and tighten mucous membranes, offering protection to the stomach lining and promoting the healing of ulcers.
tea_recipe: Peel and chop 2 ripe plantains into small pieces, then rinse them under cold water.
In a pot, combine the plantain chunks with 4 cups of water and bring the mixture to a boil.
Reduce heat and let the plantains simmer for 20 minutes, or until the liquid is reduced by half.
Strain the tea to remove the plantain pieces, then let it cool to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 1 cup of the plantain tea up to three times a day, ideally before meals, for best results in managing peptic ulcers.
tea_constituents: Allantoin: Promotes healing of damaged tissue, including ulcers, due to its anti-inflammatory and keratolytic properties.
Mucilage: Forms a protective layer over the mucous membrane, reducing irritation, inflammation, and promoting the healing of peptic ulcers.
Tannins: Have astringent properties that help to protect and heal peptic ulcers by reducing inflammation, bacterial infection, and promoting tissue regeneration.
Flavonoids: Exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, helping to protect the gastric lining, prevent ulcer formation, and promote ulcer healing.
Rosmarinic acid: Demonstrates gastroprotective effects, inhibiting gastric acid secretion, reducing the formation and promoting the healing of peptic ulcers.

other_remedies: Probiotics are the most common and effective natural remedies for peptic ulcers. These are live bacteria and yeasts that are beneficial for our health, particularly the digestive system. They work by restoring the balance of bacteria in the gut, which can be disrupted by factors such as stress, diet, and the use of certain medications like nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). Studies have shown that probiotics, such as Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, can help reduce inflammation, strengthen the gut lining, and inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria like Helicobacter pylori, all of which contribute to the development of peptic ulcers. Foods rich in probiotics include fermented foods like sauerkraut, kimchi, yogurt, and kefir, as well as probiotic supplements. It is important to consult a healthcare professional before starting any new treatment for peptic ulcers.
intro: Peptic ulcers can be soothed and healed with the help of certain herbal teas. Licorice root tea is a natural anti-inflammatory that reduces inflammation and discomfort. Slippery elm tea coats and protects the mucous membranes, speeding up the healing process. Marshmallow root tea provides a protective barrier, reducing irritation and discomfort. Ginger tea has natural anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce pain and inflammation. Drinking these herbal teas regularly can help to alleviate symptoms and promote healing.
